conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: Early in my career, I held the belief that engineering management was the natural progression for every senior developer. I thought managing people was the ultimate way to scale impact.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: That's a very common path, though the skill sets required are entirely different.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: I learned that the hard way. I hated meetings and missed coding, so I changed my mind completely. I spent years advocating that the Individual Contributor (IC) track is superior because you retain technical autonomy without organizational politics.

conversation.speaker: assistant
conversation.text: Staying close to the metal can be highly fulfilling for technical purists.

conversation.speaker: user
conversation.text: But as a principal IC, you eventually realize you hit a glass ceiling without organizational influence. I've reassessed my stance. I now believe that a fluid 'Tech Lead Manager' role, where senior folks rotate between technical leadership and people management every 18 months, is the healthiest model for engineering longevity.

query: What is the user's current belief regarding the ideal career path structure for senior engineers?
